response:
# Diagnosis or brief
Mode: create. Domain: IS. Model supplied: yes. A trust-and-safety manager selects at most 200 flagged items for manual review using risk scores and expected-harm estimates before true violation status is known.

# Proposed narrative
During a shift, the manager allocates scarce review capacity among flagged items. Reviewing an item reveals its status and permits removal if it violates policy; an unselected flag receives no intervention and remains online. Because risk ranking is imperfect, the problem is to choose the review set that maximizes expected prevented harm within the 200-review limit, not to select known violations.

# Decision structure (DFC-12)
Actor: trust-and-safety review manager. Trigger: a shift begins with more flagged items than review capacity. Choices: which flags to send to manual review. Decision-time information: risk scores, expected-harm estimates, and capacity; true violation status and review findings are unknown. Stakes: expected prevented harm. Frictions: at most 200 reviews; unselected items remain online. Mechanism: selection triggers review, which reveals status and enables removal if violating. Trade-off: reviewing one item uses capacity that could target another with different risk and expected harm. Counterfactual: alternative feasible review sets, including score-only prioritization. Model mapping summary: selected set is decision; status is latent state; score and harm estimate are observations; review finding is later observation; prevented harm is objective/outcome. Evidence status summary: score calibration is assumed. Boundaries summary: no perfect violation knowledge or intervention on unreviewed items.

# Consistency gates
Actor—pass: the manager controls review selection. Timing—pass: selection precedes review findings. Information—pass: true status remains unknown at selection. Behavior—pass: only reviewed items reveal status and enable removal. Constraints—pass: the 200-review capacity is explicit. Objective—pass: expected prevented harm matches the model. Eligible: true.

# Model mapping
flagged items selected for review | decision | review queue for the shift | chosen before findings | selection, not violation status
risk scores | observation | estimated violation risk | available at selection | imperfect ranking signal
expected-harm estimates | observation | modeled harm conditional on item | available at selection | estimate, not realized harm
true violation status | random_variable | whether item violates policy | unknown until review | manager cannot condition on it
review finding | outcome | status revealed by inspection | realized after selection | consumes capacity
review capacity | constraint | at most 200 inspections | fixed for shift | binds feasible set
expected prevented harm | objective | harm avoided through reviewed violations | maximized in expectation | not guaranteed realized prevention

# Claims
Scores calibrate violation risk — model_assumption; source: null; retain as an assumption and validate calibration. The manager knows which flags are violations — unsupported_claim; source: null; remove.

# Boundaries
The model excludes intervention on unreviewed flags, reviewer error, downstream appeals, and guaranteed harm prevention. Results depend on score and harm-estimate quality.

# Most important remaining weakness
The accuracy and calibration of risk and expected-harm estimates are not established.

# Mode result
Business problem: Which at-most-200 flagged items should be manually reviewed to maximize expected prevented harm when true violation status is learned only through review?
